translations.descricao: Osteoporosis is a bone metabolic disease characterized by low bone mass, deterioration of bone microarchitecture, and increased risk of fractures. The introduction of dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) revolutionized the diagnosis and management of osteoporosis since the 1980s. In Brazil, osteoporosis is considered a public health problem, with a high incidence of fractures in postmenopausal women. Diagnosis is based on T-score ≤ -2.5 by DXA, in addition to fracture risk assessment.
translations.sus.indicacao: DXA screening recommended for postmenopausal women ≥65 years or with risk factors such as previous fracture, glucocorticoid use, or low bone mass. Treatment with bisphosphonates or denosumab for T-score ≤ -2.5 or osteoporotic fracture.
translations.sus.populacaoAlvo: Postmenopausal women ≥65 years; women <65 years with risk factors (fracture, rickets, hypogonadism).
translations.sociedadesMedicas.indicacao: The AACE/ACE (2020) recommends DXA for women ≥65 years and men ≥70 years; pharmacological treatment for T-score ≤ -2.5 or fragility fracture. Brazilian Guidelines (2021) emphasize screening in postmenopausal women with risk. Austrian Society (2024) updates to include REMS as a non-ionizing alternative.
translations.epidemiologia.prevalencia: Global prevalence of osteoporosis in women >50 years is about 18-23%, with higher incidence in developed countries. In Brazil, it is estimated at 10-15% in postmenopausal women.
translations.epidemiologia.incidencia: Incidence of osteoporotic fractures is 1-2 per 100 women/year >50 years globally. In Brazil, vertebral fractures affect 20% of women >65 years.
translations.epidemiologia.mortalidade: Hip fractures associated with 20% mortality in 1 year. In Brazil, osteoporosis contributes to 5-10% of deaths from NCDs in the elderly.
